raga.name: Bhairav
raga.description: Named after Lord Shiva in his fierce aspect. The juxtaposition of komal Re and shuddh Ga, and komal Dha with shuddh Ni, creates a unique tension — majestic and awe-inspiring. One of the six principal ragas of the classical system.

forbidden.name: Shuddh Rishabh
forbidden.reason: Bhairav requires komal Re for its characteristic tension against shuddh Ga.

forbidden.name: Shuddh Dhaivat
forbidden.reason: Bhairav requires komal Dha, mirroring the komal Re in the lower tetrachord.

movement.aroha: S r G m P d N S'
movement.avaroha: S' N d P m G r S
pakad: d N S' N d P
movement.pakad_string: r G m P, G m d P, d N S' N d P

characteristic_phrases.context: Opening — the komal Re to shuddh Ga tension defines Bhairav instantly
The leap from Ma to komal Dha — dramatic Bhairav movement

characteristic_phrases.phrase: d N S' N d P
characteristic_phrases.context: Upper register — the d-N semitone mirrors the r-G tension

note_rules.Sa_approach: Approached from komal Re below — the semitone r→S gives Bhairav its gravity
note_rules.Re_Ga_tension: The komal Re (b2) to shuddh Ga (3) interval of 3 semitones is Bhairav's signature sound — an augmented second
note_rules.Dha_Ni_mirror: Komal Dha to shuddh Ni mirrors the Re-Ga tension in the upper register, creating symmetry
note_rules.Pa_role: Central anchor — phrases pivot around Pa between lower and upper tetrachords

vadi.role: King note — carries the raga's gravitas. Extended treatment in alaap with slow approaches and sustained emphasis.

samvadi.role: Queen note — the komal Re is the soul of Bhairav. Its relationship to Sa and Ga defines the raga's emotional landscape.

context.rasa: Vibhatsa (awe) and Bhakti (devotion) — specifically Shiva bhakti
context.aesthetic: Evokes the austere beauty of dawn — the first light breaking through darkness. The augmented seconds (r-G, d-N) create a sense of gravity and ancient power.

western_equivalent.relationship: Also called the Byzantine or Arabic scale. The two augmented seconds (b2→3 and b6→7) give it a distinctly Middle Eastern/Indian sound. Used in Flamenco as the Phrygian Dominant parent scale.
common_usage: Middle Eastern and Balkan folk music

lofi.notes: The augmented seconds add exotic colour to lofi. Works best with slower, more contemplative beats. The gravity may clash with overly bright lofi textures.

ambient.notes: Bhairav's solemn mood translates powerfully to ambient. The komal Re and komal Dha sustained over drones create deeply evocative atmospheres.

calm.notes: Bhairav is more intense than 'calm' — but at slow tempos with careful phrasing, it achieves a profound stillness.

upbeat.notes: The serious, majestic character doesn't naturally lend itself to upbeat production, though Bhairav-based trance or psytrance can be powerful.
